literal: sage, silent one; an *uṇādi* formation (man + i) conventionally connected with *mauna* ('silence') and with the root √man 'to think', hence 'the one given to (silent) thought'
invariant_definition: A *muni* is a sage: characteristically the silent or inward one, the contemplative associated with *mauna* ('silence') and the inner stillness from which that silence is taken to flow. The word is old and pan-Indian. It is attested from the Ṛgveda — the *Keśin* hymn (RV 10.136) describes the long-haired *muni* who, 'girded with the wind' (*vātaraśana*), drinks with Rudra from a cup of poison and travels the paths of the sky, an ecstatic-visionary figure — and runs through the Brāhmaṇical, epic, and śramaṇa literatures, where it names the contemplative ascetic withdrawn from ordinary life. A traditional gloss links *muni* with *mauna*: the sage is one fallen silent inwardly as well as outwardly, having gone beyond the dualities (*dvandvas*) of pleasure and pain, gain and loss, that drive ordinary speech and reaction. The term overlaps with but is not identical to *ṛṣi* (the seer of the Vedic mantras), *yati* (the restrained one), and *saṃnyāsin* (the formal renunciant); its distinctive note is interiorized silence rather than visionary sight or institutional status. In the śramaṇa traditions it is applied as an honorific to the founders — the Buddha is *Śākyamuni*, 'the sage of the Śākyas', and Mahāvīra is addressed as a *muni* — so the title is used across the Indian spectrum of realized contemplatives. Where the *Bhagavad-Gītā* and the Upaniṣads use it, *muni* names the contemplative under the aspect of inner stillness and self-mastery, the near-synonym *saṃyamī* ('the self-controlled one') marking the same figure under the aspect of restraint.

school_framing.shared_core: *Muni* names **the sage of inner silence and self-mastery — the contemplative who has stilled the dualities of ordinary reactive life** (etymologically linked to *mauna*; attested from the Vedic *Keśin* through epic and śramaṇa usage). The pan-Indian use is largely convergent: across Brāhmaṇical and śramaṇa traditions the *muni* is the withdrawn contemplative sage, and the title is applied to the founders of the śramaṇa paths.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Largely **shared**: the term is an honorific for the contemplative sage rather than a contested technical category, so usage converges more than it diverges. What varies is the surrounding soteriology — what the sage is held to have realized — not the meaning of *muni* itself, which in these contexts centres on stilled, self-mastered silence.

per_school.definition: In the Upaniṣadic and Gītā register the *muni* is the knower considered under the aspect of inner stillness: the one undisturbed in sorrow, without craving amid pleasures, free of attachment, fear, and anger, is 'called a *muni* of steady wisdom' (Gītā 2.56), and recurs as the *saṃyamī*, awake to the self while the world sleeps (2.69). Bṛhadāraṇyaka 3.5.1 ties *mauna* to the brāhmaṇa's realized condition. Śaṅkara and later commentators read the *muni*'s silence as the cessation of the inner agitation of desire, not merely a vow of verbal silence.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (muni as inner stillness of the knower)

per_school.definition: Across the wider Indian ascetic culture the *muni* is the silent contemplative renunciant, the wandering or settled sage whose authority rests on inner mastery and withdrawal from worldly entanglement. The Vedic *Keśin* hymn gives an early ecstatic form; the śramaṇa traditions make the title honorific for their founders (Śākyamuni, the Jain *muni*); and the epic-Upaniṣadic literature uses *muni* and *mauna* together for the sage who has gone beyond speech-driven desire. The term overlaps with *ṛṣi*, *yati*, and *saṃnyāsin* without being identical to them.
primary_loci: general epic-Upaniṣadic and śramaṇa usage of muni / mauna

textual_history.summary: *Muni* is one of the oldest Indian words for the religious virtuoso, and its earliest profile is strikingly unlike the later 'silent sage'. In the Ṛgveda's Keśin hymn (10.136) the *muni* is the long-haired, wind-girded (*vātaraśana*) ecstatic: clad in soiled tawny rags, 'maddened' by his muni-state (*unmaditā mauneyena*), he rides the wind, traverses the worlds, knows the thoughts of all, and drinks poison from the cup with Rudra — a visionary, shamanic figure of inspired frenzy. The link to *mauna* ('silence') is already latent here in the word *mauneya* ('the muni-condition'), but it is the Upaniṣads that make silence-and-stillness the defining note. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka has the brāhmaṇa, having exhausted learning (*pāṇḍitya*) and child-like simplicity (*bālya*), 'become a muni', and then, having exhausted both non-silence and silence (*amauna* and *mauna*), 'become a brāhmaṇa' (3.5.1) — *muni* now an inward, contemplative station, and the same Upaniṣad's refrain 'knowing this very [Self] one becomes a muni' (4.4.x, etam eva viditvā munir bhavati) ties muni-hood to Self-knowledge. The *Bhagavad-Gītā* completes the interiorisation: the *muni* is the man unshaken in sorrow, free of craving, fear, and anger, 'called a *muni* of steady wisdom' (2.56), and recurs as the *saṃyamī* awake when all beings sleep (2.69). The development is thus a real shift of register — from Vedic ecstatic to Upaniṣadic-Gītā sage of inner silence — within a continuous word; the connecting thread is withdrawal from ordinary life, recast from rapture to stilled self-mastery.

stages.sanskrit: munayo vātaraśanāḥ piśaṅgā vasate malā | vātasyānu dhrājiṁ yanti yad devāso avikṣata || unmaditā mauneyena vātām̐ ā tasthimā vayam | … | keśī viṣasya pātreṇa yad rudreṇāpibat saha
stages.rendering: The munis, girded with the wind, wear soiled tawny garments; they follow the rush of the wind when the gods have entered [them]. 'Maddened by the muni-state (mauneya), we have mounted upon the winds.' … The long-haired one, with the cup of poison, when he drank together with Rudra.
stages.note: The earliest *muni*: a wind-girded (vātaraśana), long-haired (keśin) ecstatic-visionary, 'maddened' by his muni-condition (mauneya) and companion of Rudra. This is inspired frenzy, not silence — yet the word *mauneya* already contains the *mauna* root that the later tradition foregrounds. (Aufrecht reads the plural 'munayo vātaraśanāḥ'; the singular 'munir vātaraśanaḥ' is sometimes cited. We print the verbatim on-disk plural.)

stages.sanskrit: bālyaṃ ca pāṇḍityaṃ ca nirvidyātha muniḥ | amaunaṃ ca maunaṃ ca nirvidyātha brāhmaṇaḥ
stages.rendering: Having done with both child-like simplicity (bālya) and learning (pāṇḍitya), then [he becomes] a muni; having done with both non-silence and silence (amauna and mauna), then [he becomes] a brāhmaṇa.
stages.note: The interiorising turn: *muni* is now a contemplative station reached by transcending mere learning, and it is bracketed explicitly with *mauna* — silence. Śaṅkara's on-disk gloss derives *muni* from *manana* (reflection): 'from reflection he becomes a muni, a yogin'. The same Upaniṣad has 'etam eva viditvā munir bhavati' — 'knowing this very [Self], one becomes a muni' (BrhUp 4.4) — binding muni-hood to Self-knowledge rather than to ecstatic vision.

stages.sanskrit: duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ | vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthita-dhīr munir ucyate
stages.rendering: He whose mind is unshaken amid sorrows, who is free of longing amid pleasures, from whom passion, fear, and anger have departed — he is called a muni of steady wisdom.
stages.note: The *Gītā* defines the *muni* purely by inner stillness and freedom from the reactive passions — the antithesis of the Vedic ecstatic's frenzy. Śaṅkara glosses 'muni' here as *saṃnyāsin*, the renunciant of steadied wisdom. The interiorisation begun in the Upaniṣads is complete: silence is now stilled mind, not a vow or a trance.

stages.sanskrit: yā niśā sarva-bhūtānāṃ tasyāṃ jāgarti saṃyamī | yasyāṃ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśā paśyato muneḥ
stages.rendering: What is night for all beings, therein the self-controlled one (saṃyamī) is awake; that in which [other] beings are awake is night for the muni who sees.
stages.note: The *muni* under the aspect of inversion of ordinary awareness: awake to the self while the world sleeps to it, asleep to what the world chases. Here *muni* and its near-synonym *saṃyamī* ('the self-controlled one') name one figure under the two aspects of inner seeing and restraint — the Gītā's settled portrait of the silent sage.

translator_note: 'Monk' imports Christian-monastic connotations and 'sage' can sound merely intellectual; the load-bearing nuance is silence and inner stillness (*mauna*). The title is conferred on one free of grief, craving, fear, and anger, so 'silent sage' or 'sage of stilled mind' is closer than a generic 'wise man'. Distinguish *muni* from neighbouring terms — *ṛṣi* (seer), *yati* (the restrained), *saṃnyāsin* (the renunciant) — which are related but not synonymous; and render the near-synonym *saṃyamī* as 'the self-controlled one', keeping the accent on restraint.
